Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed balanced comparative proverb; repeated adjective-noun phrase with parallel ellipsis.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The higher a person’s status or ambition, the greater the risk and responsibility.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Jabatan rea bawa tanggung jawab rea.
Usage Contexts: leadership, advice, politics, family counsel, workplace responsibility
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Mountain-and-valley imagery fits Sumbawa’s landscape and older counsel about rank, obligation, and public trust.
First Example Sentence: Lamin sia dadi ketua, ingat mo: tingi olat, tingi penyembir.
Second Example Sentence: Ama no dangu puji jabatan; tingi olat, tingi penyembir, ling nya.
Third Example Sentence: Pang boat desa, tau rea amanat kenang tingi olat, tingi penyembir.

Idiom (Sumbawa): Nya baeng isi, nya baeng ai’
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed parallel clause; repeated subject and possessive verb phrase with paired nouns.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Whoever controls a resource or position must protect the surrounding people and conditions that sustain it.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Tau baeng kuasa mesti jaga tau sekeliling.
Usage Contexts: governance, community duty, farming, management, ethical leadership
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Fish-and-water imagery reflects coastal, river, and irrigation knowledge in Sumbawa social life.
First Example Sentence: Kepala dusun ya rawat tau ode, sabab nya baeng isi, nya baeng ai’.
Second Example Sentence: Nya baeng isi, nya baeng ai’, nan pesan ama ko anak boat.
Third Example Sentence: Lamin baeng sawah, jaga ai’ ke pematang; nya baeng isi, nya baeng ai’.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed cause-result clause pair; adjectival shame phrase followed by verbal deception phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person lies or exaggerates to cover embarrassment, poverty, failure, or social insecurity.
Usage Contexts: humor, social criticism, family talk, gossip, moral advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The expression encodes village norms around dignity, scarcity, and the pressure to preserve face.
First Example Sentence: Na bola ke dengan, kangila rara, kangampang bola mo lalo rua.
Second Example Sentence: Ia beli baju utang, kangila rara, kangampang bola pang pasar.
Third Example Sentence: Kangila rara, kangampang bola no nentu bawa mulia.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase with directional complement; metaphorical action construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Doing something in an obviously foolish, upside-down, or illogical way.
Usage Contexts: workplace mistakes, household humor, teaching, criticism, technical tasks
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The image of putting trousers on from the head gives a concrete domestic metaphor for absurd method.
First Example Sentence: Mula-mula baca aturan, na satama saluar ola otak.
Second Example Sentence: Ia bongkar mesin tanpa ilmu; satama saluar ola otak ling montir.
Third Example Sentence: Keputusan nan mara satama saluar ola otak, no gita jalan.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Condensed fixed verb-object clause; hunting/chasing metaphor with omitted subject.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Acting too late after the real chance has already gone; wasted effort after delay.
Usage Contexts: missed opportunities, deadlines, warnings, farming, travel, bureaucracy
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The released pig evokes older hunting and village-life imagery where delay makes pursuit pointless.
First Example Sentence: Sesudah kapal lalo, baru sia datang; samang bawi lis.
Second Example Sentence: Data ra ilang baru tu cari, samang bawi lis ko kantor.
Third Example Sentence: Nasihat ama no ra dengar, sanopoka rugi samang bawi lis.

Idiom (Sumbawa): Patis jaran na dampi burit, patis kebo na dampi otak
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Bipartite prohibitive proverb; repeated concessive adjective plus negative imperative.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Even when someone looks safe or tame, remain cautious around unfamiliar people or risky situations.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Na terlalu percaya ko tau anyar.
Usage Contexts: travel, business caution, parenting, negotiation, public safety
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Horse and buffalo handling knowledge is used as a practical rural warning about hidden danger.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Sumbawa): [ˈpatis ˈjaran na ˈdampi ˈburit ˈpatis ˈkəbo na ˈdampi ˈotak]
First Example Sentence: Patis jaran na dampi burit, patis kebo na dampi otak, ling ina ko anak.
Second Example Sentence: Dagang anyar manis tutur, tapi patis jaran na dampi burit.
Third Example Sentence: Pang rantau, ingat patis kebo na dampi otak lamun kenal tau anyar.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed causative/placement clause; verb phrase plus prepositional complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Entrusting valuables or duties to someone who is naturally likely to misuse them.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Na serah amanat ko tau no dipercaya.
Usage Contexts: trust, finance, household management, workplace delegation, satire
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The cat guarding meat is a household metaphor for predictable temptation and poor judgment.
First Example Sentence: Uang koperasi ra serah ko tau nan; sangentok rarek ko bodok.
Second Example Sentence: Na beri kunci lumbung ko maling, sangentok rarek ko bodok.
Third Example Sentence: Ia pilih penjaga tanpa ati-ati, sangentok rarek ko bodok jadinya.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative existential clause with future-oriented verbal complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not wait for miraculous help; success requires effort and self-reliance.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mesti usaha diriq, na tunggu tulung datang.
Usage Contexts: study, farming, work discipline, motivational speech, family advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The angel image reflects Islamic vocabulary in Sumbawa moral instruction while stressing practical effort.
First Example Sentence: Lamin ingin panen, boat mo; nonda malaekat datang raboko.
Second Example Sentence: Nonda malaekat datang raboko, jadi tu bangun sanopoka mata ano.
Third Example Sentence: Anak sekolah belajar tiap bengi, sabab nonda malaekat datang raboko.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed transitive clause with place-name complement; locative cultural metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Giving someone something they already have in abundance; an unnecessary or redundant offer.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Na beri barang ko tau baeng barang nan.
Usage Contexts: gifts, trade, workplace advice, social humor, resource planning
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kuris is associated in the proverb source with salt production, making salt-giving an image of redundancy.
First Example Sentence: Beli sira ko tau Kuris nan satempu sira ko Kuris.
Second Example Sentence: Hadiahkan kopi ko tau jual kopi, satempu sira ko Kuris mo.
Third Example Sentence: Proposal nan no perlu, mara satempu sira ko Kuris pang rapat.

Idiom (Sumbawa): Uler na tarik tali, betak na beang kapate
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound negative imperative proverb; paired commands with balanced contrast.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Use balance in guidance: do not be too harsh or too permissive, especially with children.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ajar anak ke timbangan, no keras no lemah.
Usage Contexts: parenting, teaching, leadership, conflict mediation, discipline
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kite-string handling becomes a metaphor for measured discipline and careful social control.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Sumbawa): [ˈulər na ˈtarik ˈtali ˈbətak na ˈbeaŋ kaˈpatə]
First Example Sentence: Ngajar anak mesti seimbang: uler na tarik tali, betak na beang kapate.
Second Example Sentence: Ina no keras no lemah, uler na tarik tali, betak na beang kapate.
Third Example Sentence: Pang kelas, guru ingat pesan uler na tarik tali, betak na beang kapate.
